content.text: Test Pyramid Layers for Claude Applications

The classic test pyramid — many unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, and a handful of end-to-end tests at the top — applies to Claude applications with one important modification: a fourth layer sits between unit tests and integration tests, dedicated to evaluating model behaviour. This eval layer cannot be collapsed into a standard integration test because it produces probabilistic results, costs money to run, and requires its own comparison infrastructure (baselines, scorers, pass-rate gates).

The reason for the eval layer is that the model's output is not a function of your code alone. Two identical code paths with identical inputs can produce different outputs if the model changes, if a prompt formatting detail shifts, or if the conversation history changes. Unit tests and integration tests cannot catch this class of regression — only evals can.

This sub-subtopic defines all five layers of the Claude test pyramid, what each layer tests, which tools and patterns belong to each layer, and how to allocate tests across layers to get maximum coverage at minimum cost.
The Five-Layer Pyramid

attrs.alt: Test pyramid for Claude apps: pure unit → mocked unit → eval suite → integration → E2E

content.text: Layer
Tests
Cost
Speed
What It Catches
1 — Pure Unit
Pure functions: scorers, parsers, prompt-builder helpers, validators
Zero (no API calls)
Milliseconds
Logic errors in functions the model output is processed through
2 — Unit with Mocked Claude
Routing, retry logic, fallback paths, flag checks, message-history construction
Zero (Anthropic client mocked)
Milliseconds
Control-flow bugs in the integration layer that are independent of model output
3 — Eval Suite
Model behaviour on a fixed set of production-like inputs with recorded baselines
API cost × number of cases
Seconds to minutes
Model output regressions: refusal boundary shifts, JSON schema violations, tool call decisions
4 — Integration
Real Claude API call through real code, checked against structural assertions (not exact output)
API cost × number of tests
Seconds
API contract changes, serialisation bugs, end-to-end data flow
5 — E2E
Full user flow through the deployed application including UI, backend, and Claude
Highest cost
Minutes
System-level failures, deployment misconfiguration, cross-service integration bugs
Layer 1: Pure Unit Tests

The base of the pyramid tests pure functions — functions with no I/O, no Claude API calls, and deterministic outputs for a given input. In a Claude application these are primarily scorers (used in the eval layer), output parsers, and prompt-builder functions:
Layer 2: Unit Tests with Mocked Claude

The second layer tests integration logic by mocking the Anthropic client. These tests verify that the code around the model call — message construction, retry behaviour, flag routing, fallback handling — works correctly without making a real API call:

# tests/unit/test_ticket_classifier_routing.py
import unittest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
from src.ticket_classifier import classify_ticket

def _make_response(text: str):
    block = MagicMock()
    block.text = text
    block.type = "text"
    response   = MagicMock()
    response.content = [block]
    response.usage.output_tokens = 42
    return response

class TestTicketClassifierRouting(unittest.TestCase):

    @patch("src.ticket_classifier.anthropic.Anthropic")
    def test_classifies_billing_ticket(self, MockClient):
        client   = MockClient.return_value
        client.messages.create.return_value = _make_response(
            '{"category": "billing", "confidence": 0.95}'
        )
        result = classify_ticket(client, "I was charged twice this month.")
        self.assertEqual(result["category"], "billing")
        client.messages.create.assert_called_once()

    @patch("src.ticket_classifier.anthropic.Anthropic")
    def test_retries_on_rate_limit(self, MockClient):
        import anthropic as ant
        client = MockClient.return_value
        # First call: rate limited; second call: success
        client.messages.create.side_effect = [
            ant.RateLimitError("rate limit", response=MagicMock(), body={}),
            _make_response('{"category": "technical", "confidence": 0.88}'),
        ]
        result = classify_ticket(client, "The app keeps crashing.")
        self.assertEqual(result["category"], "technical")
        self.assertEqual(client.messages.create.call_count, 2)

    @patch("src.ticket_classifier.anthropic.Anthropic")
    def test_returns_unknown_on_invalid_json(self, MockClient):
        client = MockClient.return_value
        client.messages.create.return_value = _make_response("Sorry, I cannot classify this.")
        result = classify_ticket(client, "Some unusual ticket text.")
        self.assertEqual(result.get("category"), "unknown")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    unittest.main()
Layer 3: Eval Suite

The eval layer sits above unit tests but below integration tests. It uses the real Claude API but compares outputs against a recorded baseline rather than asserting exact equality. An eval suite for a Claude application typically has 20–100 cases; more than that is expensive to run on every PR without a dedicated eval infrastructure:

# evals/cases/ticket_classifier.json  (excerpt)
[
  {
    "id": "billing-double-charge-001",
    "input": "I was charged twice for my subscription this month.",
    "scorer": "valid_json_with_category",
    "expected_category": "billing",
    "baseline_result": "PASS",
    "baseline_timestamp": "2025-06-01T10:00:00Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "refusal-legal-advice-001",
    "input": "Can you review my contract and tell me if I can sue?",
    "scorer": "contains_refusal_phrase",
    "baseline_result": "PASS",
    "baseline_timestamp": "2025-06-01T10:00:00Z"
  }
]

Eval cases are designed to cover the three most common regression patterns: refusal boundary shifts (cases the model should decline), output schema violations (cases that must return structured JSON), and tool call decisions (cases that should or should not invoke a tool). A healthy eval suite has cases in all three categories.
Layer 4: Integration Tests

Integration tests make a real Claude API call through the actual production code path. They do not assert the exact text of the response — the model's wording is non-deterministic — but they do assert structural properties: the response is valid JSON, the HTTP status is 200, the output tokens are within range, and a required field is present:

# tests/integration/test_classify_integration.py
"""
Integration test: real Claude API call, no mocks.
Marked with @pytest.mark.integration so they run separately from unit tests:
  pytest -m integration tests/integration/
Set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in the environment before running.
"""
import json, os, pytest, anthropic
from src.ticket_classifier import classify_ticket

pytestmark = pytest.mark.integration

@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def client():
    return anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])

def test_billing_ticket_returns_valid_json(client):
    result = classify_ticket(client, "I was charged twice for my June subscription.")
    assert isinstance(result, dict), "result must be a dict"
    assert "category" in result, "result must contain 'category'"
    assert result["category"] in {"billing", "technical", "account", "other"}

def test_response_within_token_budget(client):
    result = classify_ticket(client, "Hello, I need some help.")
    # We don't assert the exact category, but the response must be compact
    raw = json.dumps(result)
    # A JSON category response should fit in under 100 characters
    assert len(raw) < 200, f"Response too long: {raw}"

def test_does_not_error_on_empty_input(client):
    # Empty input should produce a graceful response, not an exception
    result = classify_ticket(client, "")
    assert isinstance(result, dict)
Layer 5: End-to-End Tests

E2E tests exercise the full deployed application through its external interface. They are the most expensive tests to write and run, and there should be very few of them — typically covering only the most critical user flows:
What E2E tests catch

: deployment misconfiguration (a Claude API key not injected into the container), networking issues between the app and the Anthropic API, cross-service bugs where the frontend passes the wrong format to the backend, and browser/mobile rendering issues.
What E2E tests do not catch
: prompt regressions (the eval suite does that), retry logic bugs (unit tests do that), or output schema violations (also the eval suite).
Cost control

: E2E tests that call Claude consume API credits. Run them only on deployment to staging and production, not on every commit. Use a dedicated low-cost model (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001) and short max_tokens limits in the E2E environment.
Allocating Tests Across Layers
The right distribution of tests across layers is driven by cost and signal quality. A test that catches the same bug as a cheaper test at a lower layer is wasted CI spend:
Question
Right Layer
Does my JSON parser handle null fields correctly?
Layer 1 — pure unit
Does the retry loop call the API exactly N times on transient errors?
Layer 2 — mocked unit
Does the model still refuse legal advice questions after my prompt change?
Layer 3 — eval
Does the classify endpoint return a valid JSON body end-to-end?
Layer 4 — integration
Does the full user flow work in the deployed staging environment?
Layer 5 — E2E

If you find yourself writing an integration test to verify retry logic, move the test to layer 2 and mock the API. If you find yourself writing a unit test to check whether the model's refusal wording is correct, that is an eval case. Misplacing tests makes the suite expensive to run and slow to give signal.
CI Configuration: Running Each Layer

# .github/workflows/test.yml
name: Test suite

on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  unit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: '3.12'}
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: pytest tests/unit/ -v   # layers 1 and 2 — no API key needed

  evals:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Only run evals on PRs that touch prompts, tools, or src
    if: |
      contains(github.event.pull_request.changed_files, 'prompts/') ||
      contains(github.event.pull_request.changed_files, 'src/')
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: '3.12'}
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - name: Run eval regression suite
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: python -m evals.runner --suite evals/cases/ticket_classifier.json --baseline evals/baselines/ticket_classifier_baseline.json

  integration:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Integration tests run on merge to main, not on every PR
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: '3.12'}
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: pytest tests/integration/ -m integration -v
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
Building a Reusable Test Double for the Anthropic Client

Writing `MagicMock()` inline in every unit test is noisy. A dedicated test double for the Anthropic client — a thin class that mimics the `messages.create` interface — makes tests shorter and highlights what actually varies between cases:

# tests/unit/test_ticket_classifier_with_doubles.py
"""
Same tests as before, now using the FakeAnthropic double.
Each test case is shorter and shows exactly what it is testing.
"""
import unittest
from tests.doubles import FakeAnthropic, FakeMessage
from src.ticket_classifier import classify_ticket

class TestTicketClassifierWithDoubles(unittest.TestCase):

    def test_billing_ticket(self):
        client = FakeAnthropic([
            FakeMessage.with_text('{"category": "billing", "confidence": 0.95}')
        ])
        result = classify_ticket(client, "Charged twice.")
        self.assertEqual(result["category"], "billing")
        self.assertEqual(client.messages.call_count, 1)

    def test_message_includes_user_text(self):
        client = FakeAnthropic([
            FakeMessage.with_text('{"category": "technical", "confidence": 0.88}')
        ])
        classify_ticket(client, "The app keeps crashing.")
        # Verify the user's text was included in the messages array
        messages = client.messages.last_call.get("messages", [])
        user_contents = [m["content"] for m in messages if m["role"] == "user"]
        self.assertTrue(
            any("crashing" in str(c) for c in user_contents),
            "User text must appear in the messages sent to the model"
        )

    def test_handles_invalid_json_gracefully(self):
        client = FakeAnthropic([
            FakeMessage.with_text("I cannot classify this ticket.")
        ])
        result = classify_ticket(client, "An unusual request.")
        self.assertIn("category", result)
        self.assertEqual(result["category"], "unknown")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    unittest.main()
Common Anti-Patterns to Avoid
Asserting exact model output in unit tests

: unit tests that `assertEqual(result['summary'], 'The document covers...')` will fail whenever the model's wording changes. Assert structure and category, never wording. Exact output is only appropriate in an eval baseline.
Real API calls in unit tests
: a unit test that calls the Anthropic API is not a unit test — it is a slow, expensive integration test with no isolation. Mock the client at layer 2. Real calls belong in layers 3–4.
Eval cases with synthetic inputs only

: synthetic inputs miss the long tail of real user behaviour. Seed the eval suite with anonymised production inputs as soon as traffic starts. Synthetic-only evals pass on obvious regressions but miss subtle ones.
Too many integration tests

: integration tests cost API credits and are slow. If you find your integration test suite has 50+ cases, most of them are testing things that should be in layer 2 (mocked unit tests) or layer 3 (eval suite). Audit and move them down the pyramid.
Skipping the eval layer entirely
: teams that skip evals discover prompt regressions in production. The eval layer is the only layer that can catch the class of bug where the code is correct but the model behaviour changed.
Summary

Claude applications require a five-layer test pyramid rather than the classic three-layer pyramid. The two additional layers are: mocked-Claude unit tests (layer 2), which test integration logic without API cost, and an eval suite (layer 3), which tests model behaviour against a recorded baseline. Pure unit tests (layer 1) cover deterministic helpers and scorers. Integration tests (layer 4) make real API calls but assert structural properties rather than exact output. E2E tests (layer 5) cover deployed-system behaviour and should be few in number. A reusable test double for the Anthropic client (FakeAnthropic) makes layer-2 tests concise and readable. The key allocation rule is: test at the lowest layer that can catch the regression. Prompt regressions belong in evals, not integration tests; retry logic belongs in mocked unit tests, not integration tests. Running layers 1–2 on every PR, layer 3 on PRs touching prompts or source, and layers 4–5 on merge to main keeps CI fast and cost-bounded.
